card_text: Thank you for making the time to accept this Brief. I am speaking on behalf of my twin brother, Stephen MacDougall. He died on May 25, 2021, 4 weeks after receiving his first dose of Astra Zeneca vaccine, at the age of 45. Before I delve into details, I thought I would speak a little about who Stephen was. Stephen was beloved by so many people. To know Stephen was to experience love, and a joy for life. Stephen could best be described as a bright light. He drew people in, everywhere he went, and made people feel better for knowing him. He was magnetic. When he died, people from every part of his tremendous life reached out to extend their condolences and share their stories. Friends, teachers from every school (including substitute teachers from elementary school), co-workers, classmates, teammates, and family shared not only their love, but specific– sometimes ridiculous and funny– details. . A common thread emerged: Stephen made people feel better, he was compassionate, he was joyful, he extended a hand to all. He could bridge divides. He always assumed the best of intentions of others and had a way of making us all see each other through a different lens. A better, more compassionate lens. It was as if he was a flashlight, shining light everywhere he went. He was a devoted son, a caring brother. He was a cherished husband. His most beloved title was that of “Daddy”. He leaves behind a grieving family including

Name: I would like to share my experience of navigating life during the pandemic and the impacts of mask wearing, lockdowns and vaccine mandates. Being in the tourism industry, our business was shut down in
study_title: Emergency Situation Facing Canadians in Light of the COVID-19 Pandemic
card_text: I would like to share my experience of navigating life during the pandemic and the impacts of mask wearing, lockdowns and vaccine mandates. Being in the tourism industry, our business was shut down in 2020 and we did not know if we were going to make it. Would we lose our house if we couldn’t pay our mortgage, would we have a business once lockdowns were over, would we be able to feed out family. I can’t begin to express the stress and fear of being forced to shut down everything you have worked so hard for and not know how you would come out the other side. Being forced to wear masks, especially when there is no science showing that they are effective, added a lot of stress and inability to go certain places. Most places in my community did not honour exemptions and I felt vilified and disregarded for even asking businesses if I could enter. I would try to wear one and at times had to leave the store due to feeling faint, anxiety and unwell. I would often travel over an hour to buy groceries at a store that would honour exemptions. My son, who has asthma, was also forced to wear a mask at school and at work as apparently asthma is not considered a legitimate reason to not wear one, thus exacerbating his condition. My daughter also struggled with wearing masks and would often have to leave stores due to an inability to breathe

Name: Dear Standing Committee on Health, 05.29.2022 I am writing to you as part of an initiative to raise awareness of just how disparaging the federal COVID-19 mandates are to Canadians. I’m going to share
study_title: Emergency Situation Facing Canadians in Light of the COVID-19 Pandemic
card_text: Dear Standing Committee on Health, 05.29.2022 I am writing to you as part of an initiative to raise awareness of just how disparaging the federal COVID-19 mandates are to Canadians. I’m going to share how the mandates have impacted me in hopes that my story may help you change your position on the matter. I’m an American who moved to Canada and married a Canadian at the end of 2019. My family was sad about the move because we are very close, yet they supported me nonetheless. Of course, my husband could have immigrated to the states, but circumstantially, Canada worked best for us. The person who was most concerned about the move was my dad. He was worried that something would cause the US/Canada border to close and he wouldn’t be able to get to me. I assured him that would never happen. My parents and sister lived a short six-hour drive away in Portland and I thought I would be able to visit them whenever I want — I was wrong. A few months after our wedding, the pandemic reached Canada and the land borders were shut down to all “non-essential” travel. Among many things, family reunification was classified as non-essential and it broke my heart. The move to Canada was already difficult enough, but the border closures made it exponentially harder. I couldn’t see my family for most of 2020 because visiting the states now required a flight I couldn’t afford and two weeks of quarantine upon

card_text: To whom it may concern, I do want to briefly speak to the pandemic and the way the government has handled it all. As an unvaccinated citizen of Canada I feel my rights have been violated. I cannot freely travel by plane or train in my own country, even though I have natural immunity to the virus after having had it (with no ill effects). My job was in jeopardy after our PHO issued an edict for all employers to decide on a vaccination policy. I had to fight to keep my job, and I don’t know how much longer I will have it. I was forced to wear a mask everywhere for months on end, even though almost every study shows no appreciable benefit, and cloth masks are essentially facial decorations to virtue signal compliance. I was banned from all public activities for 8 months, even though the science clearly shows that vaccinated people spread the virus just as easily as unvaccinated. I was unable to even visit my mother in the hospital when she fell and broke several bones. Ironically, she was placed on a floor with covid patients and ended up contracting covid from someone in the hospital who was vaccinated because they don’t allow any unvaccinated people in. The government’s policies both federally and provincially have led me to believe that this pandemic response is less about following the science than it is about control and coercion. card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on the Emergency Situation Facing Canadians in Light of the COVID-19 Pandemic May 9, 2022 OVERVIEW [ORG], the national voice of Canada’s hospitals, research institutes and healthcare organizations, welcomes the opportunity to submit this brief to the Standing Committee on Health as part of its study on the Emergency Situation Facing Canadians in Light of the COVID19 Pandemic. While citizens and governments across Canada are eager to put COVID-19 behind them in favour of a post-pandemic normal, healthcare workers and organizations across the country are still in crisis. This is in part due to the strains imposed upon them by the pandemic. However, the challenges facing healthcare in Canada right now are symptoms of a much larger problem: Our health system is not built to handle modern needs and has been slowly crumbling under decades of mounting population and demographic pressure. COVID-19 simply pushed the system beyond its breaking point. One of the main worries we hear from our member institutions is that governments and the health sector will fail to act on the lessons learned during the pandemic and choose instead to lapse back into the pre-pandemic status quo once the worst of COVID-19 is past. We cannot let this happen. As a nation, we desperately need to come together and use our experiences during the pandemic – and the decades of research and experience before it – to build a system that works seamlessly across the country, for everyone. card_text: Hello, On April 28, 2022 a scientific article titled “Increased emergency cardiovascular events among under-40 population in Israel during vaccine rollout and third COVID-19 wave” was published on a renowned Nature.com website at https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598022-10928-z , two of its authors are a professor and post-doctoral fellow respectively. card_text: April 27, 2022 Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health’s Study on the Emergency Situation Facing Canadians in Light of the COVID-19 Pandemic Summary: The COVID-19 pandemic has had a dire effect on wait times for medical imaging procedures across the country. Canadians who may have postponed diagnostic and necessary follow-up imaging early in the pandemic are now in more urgent need of care. This has created a massive influx of patients attempting to reschedule their appointments, not to mention the additional treatment that will be required for those undiagnosed or with worsening conditions. If we do not take action, the increased pressure on radiology will catch up after the pandemic, the growing baseline demand for imaging, and the existing capacity limitations in radiology departments will combine to continue to damage the healthcare system. Excessive and lengthening wait times for medical imaging is reducing efficiency across our healthcare system and leading to burnout in our health workforce. Canadians are being diagnosed later into the progression of their ailments, leading to a reduction in treatment options, increased recovery time, and worsening patient outcomes. Investments must be made quickly to support more up-to-date modern imaging equipment and information technologies. Overview: Medical imaging is our healthcare system’s gateway for diagnosing diseases, and patients from every demographic rely on it to detect and treat these conditions. We are the only national not-for-profit network composed of academic health centres across the country focused on connecting required expertise and infrastructure to bring the most promising novel cancer immunotherapies to patients through clinical trials. [ORG] “makes the right tools available at the right time” to translate research and accelerate the speed at which the public will start to benefit from research investments made over many years by federal and provincial governments, NGOs and cancer charities. As it relates to the Health Committee’s study on “The emergency situation facing Canadians in light of the COVID-19 pandemic”, we are increasingly concerned that the impact of pandemic restrictions on delayed cancer diagnostics and postponed treatment for Canadians with will lead to a reduction of treatment options, as well as the need to employ more aggressive treatment. Immunotherapy harnesses the immune system to preferentially seek and destroy tumor cells, and is a revolutionary approach to treating cancer. It has the potential to replace some more aggressive treatment options, and to provide an alternate route for treatment for patients who have exhausted all other options. card_text: To whom it may concern, I implore the committee to end all Covid-19 vaccination mandates. As a fully vaccinated Canadian and a healthcare worker, I am seeing the Liberal Party completely ignore new scientific evidence, (such as the CDC reports of natural immunity being highly effective against the new Delta, and Omicron variants), for their own political gain. The informed consent part of healthcare has been largely ignored, propelled by the Liberal Party’s stubbornness to see that being against mandates is NOT the same as being against vaccines, and that being hesitant about a relatively new medical measure but proceeding with it anyway for fear of losing one’s livelihood is coercion, NOT consent. The Covid-19 lockdowns and mandates have created a mental-health crisis of epic proportions. We’re seeing suicides, domestic abuse, and substance abuse on the rise, all in consequence of the lockdown and Covid-19 vaccine mandates. This a direct result of the Liberal Government’s overreach. Enough is enough. We have to learn how to live with this virus. Please end all Covid-19 vaccination mandates.

card_text: [CONTACT] Vancouver, BC Canada, [CONTACT] Feb. 19Th, 2022 To Standing Committee on Health, I am an RN of 30 years who lost her employment because it didn't make sense to me to expose myself to an experimental COVID vaccine. Nor did I want to give it to the general public before the long term safety studies were peer reviewed and published. I watched as friends, colleagues and family members were forced into taking the COVID vaccinations to stay employed, continue to have social contact, to be able to travel and continue studies. I watched as ridiculous mask mandates were rolled out to protect people from viral particles so small they would pass right through the majority of masks. I watched as young people post-vaccination suffered from myocarditis and pericarditis and how woefully inadequate our Adverse Events reporting system is. I listened to months of CBC droning on about the COVID numbers based on a completely unreliable PCR testing system with its cycle variances. I watched as early, seemingly effective, treatment was suppressed and how Pfizer and Moderna raked in billions of dollars from their vaccines. I saw how ICU bed occupancy in BC last year was no different from previous years. I watched how millions contracted COVID despite the vaccinations. I watched the excruciating loneliness experienced so many members of the public but especially the elderly. And to top the cake our prime minister called the unvaccinated me “racist and misogynist” and was unable to have a conversation with truckers.

card_text: [ORG] Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Emergency Situation Facing Canadians in Light of the COVID-19 Pandemic February 2022 Recommendations 1. Increase specific funding for cataract surgeries and increased access to surgical operating room time. 2. Fund and provide increased access to mental health supports, specifically for physicians and allied health workers. 3. Create a Federal Vision Health Desk within Health Canada or PHAC to support the creation of a national vision strategy. Key Issues Impact of Covid-19 on surgery backlogs Jurisdictions worldwide ramped down ophthalmic surgeries to mitigate the effects of COVID-19, creating a global surgical backlog. Since the start of the pandemic in 2020, the overall estimated number of patients awaiting surgery grew by 129% and 150%, respectively. The estimated mean wait time for patients for all subspecialty surgeries in Ontario increased to 282 days in March 2021 compared with 94 days in 2019. The provincial monthly additional resources required to clear the backlog by March 2023 was estimated to be a 34% escalation from the pre-pandemic volumes (4626 additional surgeries). Cataract surgeries, one of the most common surgeries performed in Canada, has been particularly impacted. We know that cataract surgery is enormously important to patients: not only does this sightrestoring surgery increase their quality of life, but it also helps prevent falls and other injuries. The Conference Board of Canada report Ophthalmology in Canada has highlighted the importance of ophthalmological procedures and the associated costs savings to the health care system. card_text: Nasal Naloxone vs Needle-based Naloxone delivery by Non-Healthcare Professionals Introduction Since the Opioid Crisis began in North America (circa 2013) and spread across the USA, finding itself in Canada in 2014/15, death rates have climbed steadily, year-on-year. In almost every illicit “street-drug” available across North America, Opioids are mixed, or blended into these drugs to increase addiction, dependence, and potency. Countries, governments, municipalities and nonprofit frontline organizations have worked diligently to reduce mortality and morbidity. These efforts and initiatives focus on education, testing of drugs to confirm Opioid presence, and the global training of non-healthcare providers to deliver life-saving naloxone to suspected opioid overdosed individuals, prior to the arrival of EMS, and related Emergency agencies such as Fire-Rescue and Law-Enforcement. Terminology Opioids – are synthetic drugs that are not naturally-derived from the sap of the poppy plant. Opiates – are drugs made from the sap of the poppy plant, and may include some Opioids, deeming them to be semi-synthetic. Concentration It is important to create a “spectrum of concentration” when discussing Opiates and Opioids. For reference we will use morphine as our base drug and assign it a concentration of “1”. As morphine is designated with a concentration of 1, the spectrum begins less than one in fractional concentrations, and escalates into the tens of thousands. Of interest, synthetic Opioids are easier to manufacture illegally than methamphetamine for example. Context and trends in the toxic drug crisis. The toxic drug crisis continues to worsen in Canada’s largest urban centres, with the unregulated drug supply becoming increasingly more toxic and deadly, ending more lives and causing life-altering harm to many people who survive poisoning events (commonly called “overdoses”).1 As the Authorities responsible for public health in the country’s three largest cities, we share the observation that Canada is in the throes of a national emergency, and that government leadership at all levels is needed to address this crisis. In all three of our jurisdictions, drug poisoning deaths have significantly increased since 2019. In Montreal, toxic drug deaths increased from 137 in 2019 to 172 in 2022 and 182 in 2023; in Toronto, suspect drug-related deaths increased from 495 in 2019 to 740 in 2022 and 733 in 2023; 2,3 and in Vancouver, deaths increased from 250 in 2019 to 578 in 2022 and 652 in 2023.4 In addition to these deaths, toxic drug poisonings are also having significant impacts

card_text: Submission to the Parliamentary Standing Committee on Health (HESA): Prioritizing a Child and Youth Lens in the Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis Study Children’s Healthcare Canada (CHC), along with our members, urges the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) to include a child and youth-centered perspective in its study on the opioid epidemic and toxic drug crisis. Through extensive consultation with our member organizations, health providers, and leading child health experts, we recognize that this crisis has deep and far-reaching impacts on Canada’s youngest citizens—impacts that are too often overlooked in favor of adult-focused interventions. Addressing these gaps is essential, as children and youth face distinct challenges, vulnerabilities, and barriers when it comes to mental health, substance use, and addiction. We respectfully submit this brief to advocate for the inclusion of a comprehensive, evidencebased approach to children’s mental health and substance use in HESA’s study. Children’s Healthcare Canada would also be honored to convene leading child health experts to testify before the committee and provide direct insights into the unique needs of children and youth in this critical study. The Urgency of Action for Children and Youth: From our consultation with children’s healthcare providers, a clear and consistent message emerged: the current systems of care are failing children and youth. card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health - “Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis” A Rural Canada Perspective Prepared by Holly Mathias, MA PhD Candidate, School of Public Health, University of Alberta June 24, 2024 Introduction I am a doctoral candidate in the School of Public Health at the University of Alberta (Edmonton, AB). My research examines the experiences of family members who provide care/support for people who use drugs in rural Canada. Through my work, I collaborate with families, community organizations, frontline workers and people with lived and living experience of drug use in rural communities across the Prairie provinces. Before beginning doctoral studies, I lived in Nova Scotia, where I conducted nationally funded community-based research on access to substance use disorder treatment programs in Atlantic Canada. The aim of this brief is two-fold: 1) to synthesize the scientific evidence on drug poisonings within a rural Canadian context; and 2) to present evidence from people with lived/living experience of substance use, families, frontline workers and community members, as heard during a 2024 national webinar and workshop on drug poisonings in rural and remote Canada. Background Since 2016, over 42,000 Canadians have died from apparent opioid toxicity deaths (Special Advisory Committee on the Epidemic of Opioid Overdoses, 2024). Between January and September 2023, it was estimated that an average of 22 Canadians died per day (Special Advisory Committee on the Epidemic of Opioid Overdoses, 2024). I feel it is important to expand on details of the London experience as I believe that some claims that have been made are not an accurate reflection of the situation. In 2012 I recognized that there were numerous patients suffering from right-side Tricuspid Valve Infective Endocarditis. An infectious disease specialist I worked with, Dr. Kaveri Gupta, identified an increase in HIV among injection drug users despite London having a robust needle exchange program run through the Regional HIV/AIDS Connection. The number of people suffering continued to rise. In June of 2016, a public health emergency was declared by the Middlesex London Health Unit due to high rates of HIV, Hepatitis C, Infective Endocarditis (IE) and iGAS (Infectious group A strep). Infectious Disease Specialists, including the Chief of Infectious Diseases at London Health Sciences Centre, Dr. Michael Silverman and scientific researchers, including myself, were able to prove that the rates of Tricuspid Valve Infective Endocarditis were a result of injecting long-acting hydromorphone, also known as HydroMorph Contin. We demonstrated that the high rate of HIV was related to sharing injection drug use paraphernalia, specifically cookers and filters, when people were injecting long-acting hydromorphone. We met with numerous community agencies to develop a collaborative community response to address this crisis. One initiative came from London InterCommunity Health

card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health, regarding the Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada Prepared by: the [ORG] March 2024 Background In response to the ongoing opioid toxicity crisis, the provision of prescribed, pharmaceutical opioids (e.g., daily dispensed immediate-release hydromorphone) as an alternative to the unregulated opioid supply—a practice known as safer opioid supply or prescribed safer supply— was introduced as a harm reduction measure. Throughout the COVID-19 pandemic, the number of safer opioid supply programs have rapidly expanded across Canada due to the provision of short-term, pilot funding by Health Canada and the publication of emergency Risk Mitigation Guidance in some provinces. As safer opioid supply programs have expanded across the country, there has been an ongoing dialogue about the evidence regarding their safety and effectiveness. Given that some people will crush and inject their immediate-release hydromorphone, one concern that has been raised in recent years is whether there is a risk of injection-related infections among people accessing hydromorphone tablets through safer opioid supply programs. Evidence from peer-reviewed publications and grey literature program evaluations has grown over recent years, which has largely indicated that these programs result in improved clinical outcomes.1 In parallel, our group has continued to monitor trends in injection-related infections across Ontario (provincially and regionally).2 In this submission, we summarize the current state of evidence and knowledge regarding risks and prevalence of injection-related infections among people with opioid use disorder within the context of safer opioid supply programs. card_text: BRIEFING NOTE A Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Opioid Crisis in Canada - Intersection of MMIWG2S+ and the Opioid Crisis in Montreal Iskweu Project - Native Women’s Shelter of Montreal Janis Qavavauq-Bibeau and Laura Aguiar February 2024 Summary: The Iskweu Project began in 2017 as a response to the ongoing crisis of missing and murdered Indigenous women, girls, trans, and 2-Spirit (MMIWG2S+) people throughout Quebec. The Iskweu Project wishes to respond to an immediate need for assistance expressed by families and loved ones in the case where an Indigenous woman, girl, trans, or 2-Spirit person goes missing. Our aim is to reduce and ultimately eradicate the number of missing and murdered Indigenous women, girls, trans, and Two-Spirit people in Quebec; to offer support and ensure adequate response from institutions when someone goes missing or is murdered. We seek to reduce barriers in accessing support and receiving an adequate response from institutions that have historically discriminated against Indigenous peoples. A large part of our work is also supporting Indigenous women, girls, trans, and 2-Spirit people who are facing violence in order to prevent future disappearances or murders. Annually, we work with approximately 75 MMIWG2S+ family members and survivors throughout Quebec. Increasingly, we have observed the impact that the opioid crisis is having on Indigenous women facing violence and the broader MMIWG2S+ crisis. card_text: Friday, January 26, 2024 Standing Committee on Health House of Commons, Canada [CONTACT] Sixth Floor, [CONTACT], House of Commons Ottawa, ON [CONTACT], Canada ATTN: Christine Sing, Clerk of the Standing Committee on Health RE: Brief – Committee Study on “Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada” As university researchers with expertise in substance use and drug policy, we are pleased to contribute evidence to the Standing Committee on Health’s study of the “Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada.” Our submission draws attention to inequities in opioid use and overdose among Two-Spirit, lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, and other queer (2S/LGBTQ+) youth and offers direction for responsive policy and practice action. This submission leverages findings and recommendations from our team’s past and ongoing research on opioid and other substance use among 2S/LGBTQ+ youth. Specifically, we share insights from a commentary publication about overdose among 2S/LGBTQ+ people, and from two published articles related to our qualitative studies with 2S/LGBTQ+ youth (ages 15 to 30) who use drugs, one of which focuses on overdose and harm reduction, and the other on opioid use. study_title: Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada
card_text: Submission to HESA Committee Study on ‘Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis’ Prepared by: [ORG] January 10 2024 The Toxic Drug Crisis is Driven by the Illegality and Lack of Regulation of Currently Illicit Drugs Contrary to dominant narratives, rates of drug use and rates of substance use disorders (SUD) have not increased in Canada over the last decade. Nonetheless, rates of drug-related fatalities and hospitalizations have skyrocketed during this period. The overwhelming driver of this crisis is the prohibition of drugs under the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act (CDSA). Prohibition has not decreased availability or use of illicit drugs but has caused continuous shifts in Canada’s unregulated drug supply. It has created a variable and volatile drug market that poses imminent risk of fatal and non-fatal overdose to all people who consume drugs, including those who use episodically or recreationally. People are now unknowingly consuming drugs that contain multiple, often novel, substances as the direct consequence of enforcement initiatives that cause drug producers and sellers to introduce ever-more substances and potent analogues into the supply to evade detection. Solutions to the toxic drug crisis must therefore be oriented toward the responsible regulation of the drug supply. Between January 2016 and June 2023 in Canada (excluding Quebec) there were 40,642 apparent opioid toxicity deaths, now averaging 22 deaths per day, and 39,435 opioid-related and 16,863 stimulant-related poisoning hospitalizations. From January to June 2023, 80% of opioid deaths involved fentanyl from the unregulated supply, a 21% increase since 2018. While this

card_text: Submissions to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health, regarding the Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada December 21, 2023 Prepared by Safiyya Ahmad, Staff Counsel Introduction The BC Civil Liberties Association (“BCCLA”) frequently engages in work that intersects with drug policy and the drug poisoning crisis, due to the overlap between these issues and the criminal legal system, Charter rights, and human rights impacts on marginalized communities. We appreciate this opportunity to provide our perspective on the opioid epidemic and toxic drug crisis (the “Drug Poisoning Crisis”), to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (the “Committee”). The BCCLA is concerned that there has been insufficient focus on the extent to which criminal law and civil liberties issues have heightened the Drug Poisoning Crisis. In the submissions below, we address three key concerns: 1. The criminalization of drug use significantly exacerbates this crisis, due to dangers of arrest, stigmatization, and social support barriers. 2. Police presence during wellness checks frequently results in violence, which deters people from calling emergency services for assistance. 3. Involuntary treatment is a violation of drug users’ civil liberties and increases the risk of death. Beyond these submissions, we acknowledge that resolving the Drug Poisoning Crisis will require a holistic approach, centering evidence‐based research as well as the knowledge and experience of people who use drugs. card_text: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Brief on Medical Device Monitoring (Breast Implants) [CONTACT] Hello, In October 2014, I was diagnosed with cancer in my right breast. In January 2015, after starting chemotherapy treatments, I was told that I should consider a right-side mastectomy. I was shocked at this point. I thought my cancer had been removed and that I undergoing preventive treatment. During several meetings with the oncologist and radiation oncologist, it was decided that a bilateral mastectomy would be preferable for various reasons. The oncologist had me meet with the plastic surgeon because she could offer me several types of reconstruction. There was never any question that staying flat would be an option. The plastic surgeon met with me and advised me that my body situation required implants. That the only risks associated with implants were rejection and the usual risks associated with anaesthesia. I was not informed that the textured implants, the ones I received, brought with them a risk of cancer or of capsular contracture. Nor was I told that I should change them every 10 years. That I should have more regular follow-up because I had implants. I learned from a television program about the health risks implants can cause. That they can be associated, not with only one cancer, but with two and now three cancers. My implants were removed in October 2019, to my great relief. card_text: BRIEF My story in brief I had rhinoplasty and breast augmentation at the age of 23 on May 25, 1987, performed by Dr. Yvon Ménard, a plastic surgeon. I started to experience a variety of problems within the first few months, including dizziness, feeling unsteady on my feet and constant and severe vertigo. Today is May 5, 2023, and at the age of 60, the problem is getting worse, and the implants are still in place 35 years later. My health is deteriorating, and in 2019, I received a diagnosis that the implants had ruptured. I have been waiting for the past five years to have TOTAL CAPSULECTOMY surgery to remove them in order to regain my health. MODEL: REPLICON silicon breast implants CATALOGUE: 2-155 SIZE: 2 WEIGHT: 155 grams CONTROL #513327 and #545202 I urge the government and Health Canada to take prompt action to establish a confidential registry for all women who have had breast augmentation, and for these women to be heard.

card_text: To: Members of the Standing Committee (HESA) SUBMISSION ON A REGISTRY FOR MEDICAL DEVICES (BREAST IMPLANTS) The following submission details my personal story with Breast Implants and the reasons why we need a registry. In 2009, I had surgery to place Allergan textured implants (under MSP), after a double mastectomy. The only advice given at the time was my implants had a warranty of 10 years. Since the surgery, I have increasingly been unwell, with most symptoms seeming to relate to Breast Implant Illness (BII) as well as capsular contracture and pain. In my long search for medical answers for being so ill, I was ridiculed by the medical system due to a lack of knowledge. Late in 2021 while continuing to search for answers, I found a Breast Implant Illness site that mentioned implant recalls. Mine were noted as being recalled in 2019 due to incidences of BIA-ALCL cancer. I immediately contacted my original plastic surgeon and was initially brushed off. Later this surgeon indicated he must have missed me on the recall, despite still being at the same address and phone number. He stated he even involved the Interior Health Authority for assistance in his search for recalled patients. He made light of BII and removal and said many of his patients chose to leave them in, perhaps due to this minimization. I also contacted Health Canada for confirmation of the recall. Several letters and several months later (necessitating involving my MP), I finally received recall confirmation. Not

Name: Mercier, May 2, 2023 Hello, I am a 39-year-old woman who had implants for eight years. In April 2014 I got the Mentor smooth round moderate profile saline 330cc implants ref: 3501645 SN: 6739990-049 a
card_text: Mercier, May 2, 2023 Hello, I am a 39-year-old woman who had implants for eight years. In April 2014 I got the Mentor smooth round moderate profile saline 330cc implants ref: 3501645 SN: 6739990-049 and 6739990-076. I had my breast implants removed one year ago. I had to undergo surgery twice on my right breast during the first year of implantation because my right implant had a capsular contraction. Physiological inflammatory reaction of the body. I had a lot of disabling symptoms (fatigue, joint pain, digestive problems, memory problems, anxiety, night sweats, hormonal imbalance, brain fog, burning eyes, sensitivity to sound, no longer able to work out, I had to stop the work I was passionate about because my body could no longer keep up, etc.). Most of my symptoms, however, have disappeared if not greatly improved since the implants were removed. I find it very important to have an up-to-date registry not only for cancers related to breast implants that are new, but also because in my case, I had a recall on my implants and I was never contacted by the cosmetic surgery clinic that put my implants in eight years ago. It is not normal that we have recalls on our cars, but not on supposedly safe objects implanted in our bodies. In short, I sincerely hope that this will change, it is important for all people who have such surgeries. Stéphanie Dupuis

Name: Mr. Luc Thériault Member of Parliament for Montcalm, Vice-Chair of the Commitee on Health Standing Commitee on Health (HESA)
card_text: Mr. Luc Thériault Member of Parliament for Montcalm, Vice-Chair of the Commitee on Health Standing Commitee on Health (HESA) The need for a national breast implant registry in Canada Dear Mr. Thériault, I am writing to express my strong support for the establishment of a centralized national breast implant registry, administered by the government, under the OVERSIGHT OF MEDICAL DEVICES (BREAST IMPLANTS) commitee. Breast implants are class IV medical devices representing the products with the highest risk potential. (1) Oversight should be conducted accordingly. - These products create a risk of a lymphoma that is specific to breast implants, BIA-ALCL. (2) Patients should be notified quickly and formally, not be made to stumble on the information in a support group. - Other issues seem to be related to breast implants and are constantly being added. (3) If it turns out that BII (Breast Implant Illness) or another condition finally becomes medically recognized, carriers will need to be notified promptly also to make an option of discussions with their treating physician. - In particular, the registry would act as a surveillance tool for pathologies related to breast implant use. It would be used to advise patients of the symptoms to watch for and to follow up with their physician, if necessary. - Health Canada has no way of determining how many patients in Canada have received these types of implants. - Automotive manufacturers notify their customers of potential problems even if they are rare (4), yet nothing is done about devices

Name: Dear M. Luc Thériault and the members of the Standing Committee on Health,
card_text: Dear M. Luc Thériault and the members of the Standing Committee on Health, I am sharing with you my utterly frightening and horrific experience with Allergan’s textured implants in order to help underline the urgency and necessity of a breast implant registry in Canada. In 2017, I consulted with a plastic surgeon, Dr. Arthur Swift, to ask what could be done about my pronounced loss of breast tissue after more than 2 years of breastfeeding my son. He recommended Allergan textured implants and so on September 11, 2017, I had breast implant surgery (410 MM, lots 2826554 & 2816784, SN 20785345 & 20706518). At no time was I informed that in 2011, the FDA announced a POSSIBLE association between textured implants and Anaplastic Large Cell Lymphoma (ALCL) and that by 2016, a DEFINITE association was established and became widely known in the medical community. Not long after my surgery I heard a report on the news about the dangers of textured implants and asked my surgeon about it. He reassured me that the incidence of the lymphoma was estimated to be only one in 1 million. So I did my best to push it out of my mind since the surgery was over and done with. Fast forward to early March 2022 when I listened to a Montreal radio host speaking about her experience with breast implants. card_text: [ORG] Submission House of Commons Health Committee’s 2023 PMPRB Study Thank you for the opportunity to offer a rare disease innovator perspective to the committee’s current study on the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB). The [ORG] ([ORG]) is a network of 17 biopharmaceutical companies dedicated to improving the lives of patients with rare diseases by developing and commercializing treatments. During the past several years, [ORG] has actively engaged in the Health Canada and PMPRB consultation processes on both the regulations and the guidelines. [ORG] also has provided input on previous health committee studies regarding the PMPRB and rare disorders in previous parliaments. All its submissions are available on the [ORG] website (www.[ORG].ca). Background on Canadian rare disease treatment landscape Let’s begin by providing some background on the rare disease treatment landscape and some of the challenges faced by rare disease patients. Canadians affected by often severe and debilitating rare disorders already struggle to access needed treatments. This is in addition to the multiple other challenges faced by rare disease patients, including the very long journey to reach a diagnosis, small patient populations, fewer health care resources available to treat the conditions and many unknowns about the disease. For many rare disease patients, there are no medicines available yet to treat their condition. That is why it is important to ensure that those that are developed can be made available to patients who need them as soon as possible. The new rules proposed by the PMPRB up to this point

Name: May 8, 2023
card_text: May 8, 2023 Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Sixth Floor, [CONTACT] House of Commons Ottawa ON [CONTACT] Canada Subject: HESA study on the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB) Dear Honourable Members, As a not-for-profit organization that represents and promotes Ontario’s vibrant and diverse life sciences sector, [ORG] ([ORG]) has monitoried, engaged and researched the proposed changes to the PMPRB since amended regulations were first tabled in 2017. In the context of the House of Commons Health Committee (HESA) study on the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB), we are well positioned to shine light on the issue and the challenges that the Agency has presented for our members, the broader life sciences ecosystem and patients. As a science-based organization, [ORG] believes in evidence-based policymaking, and for this reason we have undertaken and supported research on the reforms over the past several years. Most recently, in 2022, [ORG] commissioned research from IQVIA – a global leader in health data and analytics – to examine new medicine launches in Canada to see whether and to what extent the new rules are having an impact. The data showed that the number of new drugs launched in Canada (i.e., not just submitted to Health Canada for approval but actually sold in Canada) declined steadily after the reforms were first conceived back in 2016, whereas global launches increased on average. From 2017-2021, there were an average of 34 annual new medicine launches globally but an average of just 20 per year in Canada.1 IQVIA

Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health: Patented Medicine Prices Review Board [ORG] April 25, 2023
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health: Patented Medicine Prices Review Board [ORG] April 25, 2023 Standing Committee on Health: Patented Medicine Prices Review Board Introduction: The [ORG] ([ORG]), a national alliance of 29 patient organizations together representing millions of patients, welcomes the opportunity to provide input to the Standing Committee on Health as it considers the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB), and thanks the committee for its consideration. The [ORG] has been an active participant in consultations on pharmaceutical pricing regulation reform and the PMPRB since modernization efforts were initiated by Health Canada in 2016. This submission follows previous input into the Standing Committee on Health as part of its PMPRB study in late 2020. Name: 28 April 2023 Submitted via email [CONTACT] CAPDM Submission to the Parliamentary HESA Committee re: PMPRB
org: Canadian Association for Pharmacy Distribution Management
card_text: 28 April 2023 Submitted via email [CONTACT] CAPDM Submission to the Parliamentary HESA Committee re: PMPRB This submission is made on behalf of members of the Canadian Association for Pharmacy Distribution Management (CAPDM), in advance of the May 2, 2023 HESA Committee meeting to inform the study of the PMPRB. CAPDM is the national trade association for distributors that supply pharmacies and hospitals with over 95% of medicines consumed in Canada. Pharmaceutical distributors ensure the safe, secure, and timely access of prescription and over-the-counter medications to every corner of the nation, servicing over 12,500 pharmacies and hospitals. This efficient, accurate, and reliable supply chain ensures physical accessibility to medicines for all Canadians. CAPDM is supportive of the Committee’s decision to study the ongoing PMPRB reforms, which will have a significant unintended negative impact on core funding for Canada’s medication supply system. If these reforms are to proceed in their current format, they risk further destabilizing an already fragile drug supply system, making access to medications for Canadians, particularly those in rural and remote areas, more challenging. In light of the recent changes in leadership at the highest levels of PMPRB, we believe now is an opportune time to revisit the intentions of the proposed PMPRB reforms and hold an open discussion regarding the effects on the already-fragile drug supply and potential further weakening of infrastructure that enables national medication access. Price Compression and Impacts on Pharmaceutical Distribution CAPDM members strongly support medicine affordability, but not at the expense of accessibility.

Introduction [ORG] ([ORG]) is a professional organization dedicated to the improvement of care for women with gynecologic cancers. Since our establishment in 1979, our mission has been to promote excellence in the care of women with gynecologic cancers through education, research, and advocacy. We are submitting this brief to support the Coalition for Responsible Healthcare Guidelines in their call for a review and urgent remedy of the current guidelines on Human Papilloma Virus (HPV) screening. Our mission to improve the care of women with or at risk of gynecologic cancer by raising standards of practice, encouraging ongoing research, promoting innovation in prevention, care, and discovery, and advancing awareness. [ORG] also seeks to disseminate knowledge to practitioners, patients and the general public on gynecologic cancer as well as cooperate with other organizations committed to women’s health care, oncology, and related fields. 2. A high-risk HPV infection can lead to changes in the cells of the cervix, which can develop into cervical cancer if they are not found early. Getting vaccinated against HPV is the best way to eliminate the risk of HPV infection, as well as reduce the likelihood of several cancers. Alzheimer disease and dementia are women's issues In high-income countries, for roughly every 1 male that is diagnosed with dementia, 2 females will be diagnosed. card_text: SUBMISSION TO THE HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH WOMEN’S HEALTH STUDY January 25, 2024 SUBMITTED BY [ORG] Please address any correspondence to: Tania Vrionis, Chief Executive Officer [CONTACT] [CONTACT] INTRODUCTION Ovarian cancer is the most fatal women’s cancer, with a five-year survival rate of only 44%.1 This means that of the eight women a day who are diagnosed with ovarian cancer in Canada only four will survive five years. There is no screening test for ovarian cancer and no vaccine to prevent it. Symptoms are non- specific (e.g., bloating, abdominal pain/discomfort, urinary symptoms, changes in bowel habits) and diagnostic pathways and tools are limited making timely diagnosis extremely challenging. Ovarian cancer is not one disease. In fact, it is several different diseases making the path to scientific discoveries and improved treatments particularly complex. With very few exceptions, available treatments have not changed significantly since the 1990s. For the 3,100 Canadians diagnosed with ovarian cancer this year, they will be treated with the same methods, surgery and chemotherapy, and unfortunately these methods are not successful in most patients. Until recently, breast cancer research in Canada has received six times and prostate cancer research over two times the funding that has been invested in ovarian cancer research, despite a comparatively worse prognosis.2 It is not surprising that outcomes for ovarian cancer have not improved much in 50 years. Effective and strategic research investment is a core lever for change and a focus of this submission, but other opportunities for improving

card_text: Submission House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study The Impacts of Canada’s Breast Screening Guidelines: One Patient’s Experience Carolyn Holland, BRM My name is Carolyn Holland and I was diagnosed with Stage IIB synchronous bilateral breast cancer at age 43. I am writing with the hopes that my experience can save other Canadian women from the travesty that is the Canadian breast screening guidelines. One night in 2021, I found a lump in each breast in the shower—I had never been told about mammography in the 40s by my family doctor and self-exam was never suggested or encouraged. To add insult to injury, I had to wait more than three months for a mammogram and ultrasound following my discovery of the two lumps. After that, however, things moved quickly. Within the span of the subsequent month, I was given additional imaging, underwent multiple biopsies, and received the diagnosis. Although I knew based on my reports it wasn’t good, I still wasn’t prepared to actually hear the words from my breast surgeon. A month later, I had my first chemotherapy session, five days before Christmas. I underwent seven more rounds of chemo. For rounds 5 and 6, I was given Paclitaxel but I had severe allergic reactions both times and so my oncologist switched me to Taxotere. I now have neuropathy and permanent chemotherapyinduced alopecia. I am also at risk of a life-threatening allergic reaction if I am exposed to the same solvents that made Paclitaxel a problem

My name is Jennifer Borgfjord. I'm writing to the Standing Commitee on Health to share my cancer experience with healthcare decision-makers to call for change in breast screening guidelines in Canada. My breast cancer story is one of dense breasts and possibly a tum our developing in my late 40s. Up until May 19, 2023, I was considered not to have an increased risk for breast cancer, according to the Canadian Task Force Guidelines; no family history, no genetic mutations, and no previous chest radiation. The only subtle indication was that throughout a series of medical appointments over several decades, I was told I had "lumpy" breasts. My first mammogram letter reported a confusingto-me statistic on my breast density, but the letter for my next mammogram made it clear about my breast density, the risk factors, and that mammograms do not detect all cancers. My most recent mammogram, in March 2023, and probably the one before it, in September 2020, did not pick up a malignant ~2 cm tumors or the Ductal Carcinoma in Situ later noted in my surgical pathology report. On April 24, 2023, I found the lump by self-checking one month after I received a letter from the screening program saying my results were "normal." A very long fifteen days unfolded. I’m a wife and mother of two young daughters, now 11 and 13, living in Nova Scotia. I work full time as a corporate clerk in a busy law firm in downtown Dartmouth. Fortunately, in Nova Scotia women can self-refer to start mammograms at age 40. card_text: Submission To the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study The Harmful Effects of Current Breast Cancer Screening Recommendations Submitted In Memory of My Sister Sharon Olson By Marj Olson Women need breast cancer screening starting at age 40 and they need to be carefully informed of the risks caused by breast density. In addition, supplemental screening for women with dense breasts is crucial to ensure that cancer isn't being hidden by the dense breast tissue as it was in my sister’s case. Currently, the Canadian guideline recommends against supplemental screening for women with dense breasts. Our family has seen the tragic results. Sharon Olson was born May 10, 1962 and died August 5, 2022. She was Lois and Roland Olson’s fifth of seven children. After being diagnosed with breast cancer in November of 2017, Sharon thought back to why she hadn’t gone for regular screening mammograms. As a teacher, it was difficult to find time during business hours to call and make an appointment and then drive into the city to go to the appointment. Since her mammograms were “grossly unremarkable” and because we didn’t have a close family tie to breast cancer, it did not seem to be worth the effort to continue. When Sharon found a lump in her armpit her doctor sent her for a mammogram which found nothing due to the extreme density of her breasts, but the subsequent ultrasound found a large tumor. Name: Women’s Health Study House of Commons Standing Committee on Health How Canadian Breast Screening
card_text: Women’s Health Study House of Commons Standing Committee on Health How Canadian Breast Screening Guidelines Impacted My Diagnosis Sherry Wilcox, Hon. B.A., LL.B. My name is Sherry Wilcox. I’m writing to the Standing Committee on Health to share my story because I do not want other Canadians to go through what I went through. I recall asking about a mammogram when I turned 40 and was told by my family doctor that I was too young, that my breasts were too dense, and that screening didn’t start until I turned 50 anyways. From my experience and those of my contacts, the rhetoric espoused by the Canadian Task Force on Preventive Health Care around shared decision-making does not reflect what’s actually happening in doctor’s offices. I was not asked about my values and preferences. I was simply denied a mammogram. At the age of 44, I felt a small lump in my right breast very close to my underarm. My physician examined me and suggested it was likely a benign fibroadenoma. That was reassuring, but I requested a mammogram and was told the same thing that I was when I was 40: that I was too young, that my breasts would be too dense and, consequently, that they likely wouldn’t see anything anyways. After pushing a bit more, my doctor agreed to send me for an ultrasound. We call for more coordinated, systematic and sustained efforts to ensure accessibility to reproductive justice is broadened for women and gender diverse people with precarious immigrant status so that people of all diverse identities truly can decide what to do with their bodies. card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health The Urgent Need for Universal Coverage of Prescription Contraception Submitted by the [ORG] Please address any correspondence related to this brief to Heather Olszewski at [CONTACT] Purpose This brief describes the urgent need for universal coverage of prescription contraception in Canada. With approximately 40% of pregnancies in Canada being unplanned1, there is a significant and measurable cost burden to the health care systems that is quite preventable. Introduction As a national professional organization of experts in women’s health, The Society of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists of Canada ([ORG]) believes that access to contraception is essential for gender equity. Contraception allows women to have a reproductive life plan and to time their pregnancies. This in turn means that they are more likely to complete their educational goals, participate more fully in the workforce, enjoy more economic stability which benefits other children in the home, and have healthy pregnancies and carry to term when they do choose to have children. Financial barriers can limit the choice of contraception, leaving individuals to choose the cheapest method - not necessarily the most effective - or to use no method of contraception at all. What does this cost the health care system? Most unintended pregnancies do not result in abortion; approximately 57% will result in a birth1. The direct cost of unintended pregnancies in Canada is $320 million per year2. This analysis does not take into consideration any downstream costs to the individual and society. The

card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Brief on Women’s Health December 2023 www.womensrightsmatter.ca | [CONTACT] This brief uses the term “woman” to mean adult human female and “man” to mean adult human male, while gender non-conforming male (“GNC males”) refers to males who identify as transwomen or non-binary, and “gender” is the societal expectations based on an individual’s sex. Introduction It was traditionally believed women’s health mostly mimicked men’s health. Along with the view that using women as research subjects is problematic because of fluctuation from menstrual cycles, resulted in a limited number of females as research subjects. This has caused a major disparity between men’s and women’s medical research. Heart attacks, for instance, present differently in women than men. While there has been knowledge about male heart health since the early 1900s, it wasn’t evident until the late 2000s that heart attacks present differently in women. To this day, just 1 in 3 research subjects are women (Heart and Stroke Foundation 2018, p.4). Recent research has shown that treatments that work for men can detrimentally impact women. In other fields experiments have sometimes been carried out without considering sex in scientific research. Scientists have often used only one sex (generally male) for experiments and applied the findings to both sexes, without solid grounds. These kinds of inadvertent extrapolations might cause unintentionally harmful results to the neglected sex and economic loss (Lee, p. 167). A 2009 survey analyzed sex bias in journal articles published in 10

card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Consultations on Women’s Health Heart & Stroke November 30, 2023 Background Canada’s healthcare system is faced with a continued lack of awareness and understanding around women’s heart and brain health. Two-thirds of participants in clinical trials on heart disease and stroke have historically been men, and when women are included, sex and genderbased analysis and reporting (SGBAR) is not always conducted or included in reported results. Half of women who experience heart attacks have symptoms go unrecognized, and they are less likely than men to receive the treatments and medications they need or receive them in a timely manner. Women represent more than 50% of our population; it is time this massive gap is adequately addressed. Women are key contributors to societies and our economy; yet endure hardships that can lead to chronic disease risk and even to cardiac arrest, partly due to their important supportive role to society that they play. Efforts to improve and safeguard women’s health will contribute to a more resilient economy and society. At Heart & Stroke (H&S), we strive to live in a world where all women receive the care they need when it comes to heart and brain health. Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Endometriosis in Canada
org: Canadian Society for the Advancement of Gynecologic Excellence / EndoAct Canada / The Endometriosis Network Canada
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Endometriosis in Canada Submitted by Kate Wahl, Katie Luciani, and Liane Belland Questions concerning the content of this brief should be directed to: [CONTACT] [CONTACT] [CONTACT] Purpose This submission describes the challenges facing women, transgender, non-binary, and twospirit people living with endometriosis in Canada. It also highlights opportunities for the federal government to support the more than 1 million Canadians who have this disease by leading coordinated action to ensure timely diagnosis and management. Endometriosis affects 1 in 10 Canadian women Endometriosis impacts at least one in ten women and unmeasured numbers of transgender, non-binary, and two-spirit individuals. It is a complex and debilitating chronic disease that occurs when tissue that is like the lining of the uterus implants outside of the uterus to form lesions, cysts, and deep nodules. These growths are inflammatory and can be invasive, resulting in severe distortion of pelvic anatomy and infiltration of the abdominal cavity. Although endometriosis is commonly thought of as a gynecological disease, growths are often found on non-gynecological structures such as bowel, ureters, bladder, diaphragm, and even lymph nodes and the lungs.1 Left unmanaged, invasive endometriosis can result in life-threatening complications including bowel obstruction and organ death. There is no definitive cause or a known cure for endometriosis. Symptoms of endometriosis often begin in adolescence and include, but are not limited to, severe menstrual pain, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility as well as whole-body symptoms like fatigue.

Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Women’s Health My Journey with Endometriosis Submitted by Maggie Archibald
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Women’s Health My Journey with Endometriosis Submitted by Maggie Archibald Dear Health Committee, Firstly, thank you for the opportunity to submit a brief. I am writing to bring attention to a health issue that affects millions of individuals, including myself, and to advocate for increased awareness, support, and resources for those impacted by endometriosis in Canada. As somebody with endometriosis, I have experienced firsthand the impact this chronic condition can have on individuals' lives. The debilitating pain, menstrual irregularities, and other associated symptoms significantly affect my daily activities and overall well-being. Endometriosis is a gynecological condition that affects approximately 1 million people in Canada -- 1 in 10 women and unmeasured numbers of transgender and gender-nonconforming people1. In spring 2022, I began attending the IWK’s Endometriosis and Chronic Pelvic Pain Clinic, the first of its kind in Atlantic Canada. Over the next eight months, I tried various treatments for my endometriosis symptoms until I concluded that surgery was the best option. In February 2023, I underwent surgery, which confirmed a diagnosis of endometriosis. My journey to a diagnosis of endometriosis took about 16 years. However, individuals typically spend seven to nine years discussing symptoms and pushing for answers until they get diagnosed. There is currently no known cure for endometriosis. While various treatments and interventions exist to manage symptoms and improve quality of life, none offer a cure. Living with endometriosis is an ongoing battle—navigating through pain, uncertainty, and societal misconceptions.

Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health National Screening Guidelines and Canadian Women’s Healthcare
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health National Screening Guidelines and Canadian Women’s Healthcare A brief submitted on behalf of Responsible Healthcare Guidelines by: Shushiela Appavoo MD FRCPC, Associate Clinical Professor, University of Alberta Department of Radiology and Diagnostic Imaging [CONTACT] What are medical guidelines? Clinical guidelines are recommendations on how to diagnose and treat specific medical conditions. Guidelines are meant to ensure that patients receive appropriate diagnosis, treatment, and care. The Canadian Task Force on Preventive Health Care is an arm's length body, funded by Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC). It develops medical screening guidelines that are used to inform clinical practice across the country. Why are trustworthy national screening guidelines important to women’s health? Screening for early signs of common, significant and treatable diseases is known to decrease the incidence of advanced illness and death for many conditions. Several of the guidelines produced by the Canadian Task Force are particularly pertinent to women’s health. These guidelines include cancer screening (breast, lung, cervix) and non-cancer conditions (impaired vision, reproductive mental health). Inadequate screening results in avoidable late-stage disease, which has a negative impact on both quality of life and length of life. Many people assume that the national guideline body is led by experts who are pertinent to the conditions of interest. For example, people frequently assume that breast cancer experts are fully involved and even lead the guideline panels for breast cancer screening. Unfortunately, this is not true. The Task

It also provides an overview of the current landscape of abortion access in Canada’s North and outlines the role of birthworkers— specifically, abortion doulas—in providing abortion support. Birthworkers practice holistically by providing physical, emotional, mental, spiritual, and informational support for the pregnant person and their family. Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Antimicrobial Resistance [ORG] & [ORG]
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Antimicrobial Resistance [ORG] & [ORG] February 2026 Overview Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is one of the most urgent threats to global and Canadian health, undermining the effectiveness of life-saving medicines and straining health systems. Drug-resistant tuberculosis (DR-TB) is a major driver of this crisis, causing substantial mortality worldwide. Although Canada’s TB burden is relatively low, cases have surged sharply, with DR-TB rising by 233% over the last 14 years, exposing vulnerabilities in surveillance, diagnostics, and treatment systems and amplifying domestic risk from global transmission. TB and AMR are deeply interconnected: both require robust infrastructure for detection, stewardship, and treatment. Tackling TB is therefore essential not only to control this deadly disease but also to strengthen Canada’s broader response to AMR and reinforce overall health system resilience. Key recommendations: 1. Integrate TB into the National AMR Framework Integrating TB will help leverage existing systems, prevent duplication, and promote faster, more effective antibiotic use. 2. Invest in Research and Development for TB and AMR Funding vaccines, diagnostics, and better treatments will reduce resistance and strengthen health systems and AMR preparedness globally and domestically. 3. Strengthen Global Health Partnerships and Investments Investing and aligning TB and AMR efforts will reduce international transmission of resistant strains while reinforcing domestic resilience. 4. Build resilient diagnostic, surveillance, and stewardship systems Using TB systems as a tracer for AMR to expand diagnostics, surveillance, stewardship, and equitable access, will create a model for a resilient and effective AMR response. 5. Promote health

card_text: Leveraging Economics and the One Health Approach to Unlock the Full Potential of the Pan-Canadian Action Plan on Antimicrobial Resistance Prepared by: François Arès,1 Caroline Raymond,2 Nicolas Macia3 and François M. Castonguay4 For: The House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Presented by: François M. Castonguay Executive Summary Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) poses a major threat to Canada’s health and economy. It is already the cause of thousands of deaths each year, and without stronger action, its health burden could more than double by 2050. The economic costs could rise from a few billion dollars today to tens of billions in 2050, which would be increasingly borne by sectors outside the health care system. The Pan-Canadian Action Plan on Antimicrobial Resistance provides a solid foundation, but to be fully effective, it must be part of an integrated framework such as the “One Health” approach, which links human, animal and environmental health. Combined with economics tools, this framework becomes a powerful lever for targeting efficient interventions, strengthening intersectoral cooperation, and directing investments based on their expected health and economic benefits. The Driver of Antimicrobial Resistance Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a cross-sectoral issue affecting human and animal health. It results from the widespread use of antimicrobials in human medicine and agriculture (livestock production, dairy farming and aquaculture), with possible dissemination via wastewater, soil, the food chain, wildlife or vectors [1]. Any use can promote the emergence of resistant pathogens, a well-documented phenomenon. While AMR is not a new issue, it is currently occurring

card_text: #200, [CONTACT] SE Calgary, AB [CONTACT] Tel [CONTACT] Fax [CONTACT] Standing Committee on Health Sixth Floor, [CONTACT] House of Commons Ottawa ON [CONTACT] February 9, 2026 Re: Input for study on antimicrobial resistance Dear Members of the Standing Committee on Health, On behalf of the National Cattle Feeders’ Association (NCFA), thank you for the opportunity to provide input into your study on antimicrobial resistance. NCFA represents cattle feeders across Canada and advocates for policies that support animal health and welfare, food safety, and the long-term sustainability of the Canadian beef sector. As the Committee examines the drivers of antimicrobial use and resistance in Canada, it is important that the role of cattle feedlots is accurately understood and appropriately reflected in your analysis and recommendations. Canadian feedlots operate within a robust regulatory and stewardship framework and are subject to oversight by Health Canada and the Canadian Food Inspection Agency. Medically important antimicrobials are available by prescription only and are used under veterinary oversight through established veterinary-client-patient relationships, supported by record-keeping requirements and mandatory withdrawal periods to protect public health. Feedlots provide a highly controlled environment that enables close monitoring of cattle health, early disease detection, and targeted treatment when animals become ill. This structure supports responsible antimicrobial use by ensuring treatments are timely, precise, and based on veterinary guidance. Prompt treatment of sick animals is essential to maintaining animal welfare and reducing the spread of disease within a population, and should not be characterized as routine or indiscriminate antimicrobial use. Name: [ORG] The Essential Role of Veterinarians in Antimicrobial Stewardship in Canada For the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health January 2025
card_text: [ORG] The Essential Role of Veterinarians in Antimicrobial Stewardship in Canada For the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health January 2025 The [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits this brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health for its study on antimicrobial resistance (AMR). The aim is to inform the Committee about the role of veterinarians in antimicrobial stewardship (AMS), the system-level challenges they face, and opportunities to strengthen Canada’s public health response. Public Health Context: AMR and One Health AMR threatens human and animal health, food systems, and economic stability. In Canada, approximately 26% of human infections are resistant to commonly used treatments, with projections indicating rising resistance and increasing mortality and costs if additional action is not taken. AMR arises from complex interactions among humans, animals, and the environment. While antimicrobial use in both human and veterinary medicine contributes, transmission pathways vary by pathogen and setting. Evidence shows that a coordinated, cross-sectoral One Health1 approach is effective to reduce AMR while protecting animal welfare, public health, and the environment. Canada has established foundations through the Pan-Canadian Action Plan on AMR (PCAP) and integrated surveillance programs. However, assessments by the Auditor General of Canada and the Canadian Academy of Health Sciences identify gaps in coordination, coverage, and sustained investment. Veterinarians are essential partners in ensuring antimicrobial stewardship and protecting public health. Name: Evidence Brief: Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) in Canada, with a focus on infection control and surveillance needs in human healthcare systems. Prepared by Dr. Kevin J. Stinson, PhD CIC, on behalf of
card_text: Evidence Brief: Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) in Canada, with a focus on infection control and surveillance needs in human healthcare systems. Prepared by Dr. Kevin J. Stinson, PhD CIC, on behalf of Infection Prevention and Control Canada Submission to House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Antimicrobial Resistance Meeting Date 02 December 2025 Submitted 16 December 2025 Executive Summary Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a global health concern already associated with significant mortality and financial impacts in Canada and abroad. AMR has been described as a “silent pandemic”, as cases and transmission often go unnoticed. Antimicrobial resistant organisms (AROs) have an ability to transfer resistance genes to bacteria of the human microbiota, thereby driving persistent colonization. Today, nearly one in 10 acute care patients in Canada is colonized with an ARO. Infection control, surveillance, and antimicrobial stewardship are three of the core pillars required to effectively combat AMR. Yet there are inconsistencies with the implementation of these programs across Canada, especially in underserved communities or healthcare sectors. Current surveillance programs in Canada act as sentinels to identify broad AMR trends but lack the depth or breadth needed to effectively map transmission, and current practices lack the granularity needed to inform local practices. Opportunities to address these challenges include building infection control capacities within the healthcare workforce, through training and capacity-building initiatives such as the IPAC Hub program currently operating across Ontario. As a long-term strategy, Canada must improve our AMR surveillance programs. We need an integrated, multi-sector approach, with a

card_text: Brief and Speaking notes for Dec 11, 2025 HESA meeting. Prepared by Kim Neudorf patient and family partner with [ORG] ([ORG]). Madam Chair and committee members thank you for this opportunity to share our recommendations. I represent [ORG]. We are a non-for-profit volunteer-driven organization representing patients and families, who have experienced harm within our publicly funded healthcare system. Our mission is Every Patient Safe. We view AMR, sepsis and healthcare associated infections, as a key patient safety issues. We collaborate on AMR research, help develop standards and resources, raise public and professional awareness, present to health professionals, and participate in policy discussions. Appendix A, outlines the breadth of our work over the past 12 months. With the family’s permission, I would like to share an experience that illustrates the profound human tragedy behind the science, and the clinical, economic and social burden of AMR. A healthy 70-year-old sustained a simple foot fracture. Within two days she developed severe pain beneath the cast, accompanied by concerning changes in her vital signs and cognition. When the cast was removed, her foot was gravely infected. Sepsis was eventually diagnosed. MRSA, the most common HCA AMR pathogen, was identified. What followed was 419 consecutive days of hospital care. She lost her foot, and averted amputation of her arm by two hours. Sepsis returned, as it does, and ultimately MRSA and sepsis claimed her life. The estimated healthcare costs incurred exceeded $750,000. card_text: On the Front Line of Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Canadian Pharmaceutical Manufacturers and Exporters Alliance ([ORG]) Executive Summary Over the past two decades, domestic drug manufacturing has steadily declined in Canada. Today, Canada imports more than 80% of its medicines, leaving our healthcare system increasingly vulnerable to global supply disruptions, geopolitical instability, and foreign policy decisions beyond our control. This level of dependence is not only a public health concern - it is a national security risk that demands policy action and leadership. At the same time, the United States is moving rapidly to reshore pharmaceutical production and secure its domestic supply chain. In April 2026, the U.S. announced Section 232 tariffs on pharmaceutical imports, including potential 100% tariffs on patented medicines from countries without bilateral agreements. Canada is directly exposed, putting more than $9 billion in annual pharmaceutical exports at risk. The 232 tariffs are being reinforced by a coordinated set of domestic incentives designed to attract pharmaceutical manufacturing, including: • Regulatory prioritization for U.S.-based manufacturers • Reduced user fees, lower inspection costs, and market access priority for domestic producers • Targeted tax and investment incentives • Procurement policies favouring domestically produced medicines Canada now faces a critical inflection point. Without a clear and immediate policy response, investment will continue to shift to jurisdictions that offer stronger incentives and more predictable market conditions. Under current conditions, Canadian pharmaceutical manufacturers will face increasing pressure to move production out of Canada. Canada must act with

card_text: STRENGTHENING THE DRUG CHAIN: FOR REALISTIC AND SUSTAINABLE PHARMACEUTICAL SOVEREIGNTY Joint brief submitted to the House of Commons of Canada Standing Committee on Health as part of its work on pharmaceutical sovereignty. In a context marked by weak supply chains, cost pressure, regulatory burdens and persistent shortages, the objective should not be complete self-sufficiency, but rather pragmatic pharmaceutical sovereignty, based on resilience, diversification and secure access to drugs. The brief emphasizes that Canada cannot relocate all pharmaceutical production, but it can strengthen certain strategic capacities where they would have real added value. It also reiterates that public policies must remain consistent with the objectives pursued. In this respect, pressure on the price of generic drugs, the increasing cumbersome regulatory framework and recurrent shortages are weakening the entire drug chain, including distributors and patients. Name: [ORG] Submission to HESA Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty May 5, 2026
card_text: [ORG] Submission to HESA Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty May 5, 2026 [CONTACT] | www.canadiangenerics.ca Introduction Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty begins with the generic and biosimilar medicines industries, which supply more than 80% of the prescription medications needs of Canadian patients. As a starting point, pharmaceutical sovereignty will require decision makers to recognize that cost-saving generic and biosimilar medicines are not just budget line items — they are strategic assets that must be treated as health, economic, and national security priorities. Pharmaceutical sovereignty is about protecting the health of Canadians through a reliable, secure, and sustainable domestic supply of medicines. It means ensuring that Canada has the domestic capacity and policy frameworks in place to reliably supply and produce essential medicines to meet the needs of Canadians. The COVID-19 pandemic exposed the fragility of global pharmaceutical supply chains. Border disruptions, export restrictions, shortages of active pharmaceutical ingredients, and geopolitical tensions revealed a simple truth: when supply chains fracture, Canadians are vulnerable. Pharmaceutical sovereignty will require government decision makers to prioritize actions to address the significant existing barriers to market entry for generic and biosimilar medicines. This is needed to both ensure a robust pipeline of new generic and biosimilar medicines for Canadians and to support domestic generic drug manufacturers who want to grow their manufacturing and R&D capacity here in Canada.
