Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on Pharmaceutical Sovereignty
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on Pharmaceutical Sovereignty [ORG] May 5, 2026 Recommendations 1. Develop and implement a pan-Canadian research ethics system 2. Improve Canada’s Drug Approval Pathway 3. Establish robust and sustainable clinical trial funding to make Canada the world’s fastest country to launch a clinical trial 4. Improve post-approval drug access pathways Executive Summary Pharmaceutical sovereignty cannot be achieved through domestic manufacturing alone. For life-saving medicines (particularly oncology therapies), Canada’s long-term security, affordability, and access depend on anchoring investment across the full medicines lifecycle, from discovery and clinical trials through to production and patient access. The [ORG] ([ORG]) urges the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) to recognize that a robust, internationally competitive clinical trials ecosystem is a foundational pillar of pharmaceutical sovereignty. Countries that succeed in hosting trials are more likely to attract downstream investment and ensure early and reliable access to innovative medicines for patients. In addition, pharmaceutical sovereignty cannot be achieved if medicines are approved but remain inaccessible to patients. This includes efficient health technology assessments (HTAs), coordinated price negotiation and timely and consistent public reimbursement across Canada. When access to essential medicines is delayed, fragmented, or dependent on ad hoc mechanisms, Canada remains vulnerable to external decision-making, market instability, and supply disruptions. A sovereign pharmaceutical system must ensure approvals lead reliably to timely, funded, and equitable patient access across the country. Name: Strengthening Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Through Domestic Supply and Competitive Post-Patent Markets
card_text: Strengthening Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Through Domestic Supply and Competitive Post-Patent Markets Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) [ORG] May 2026 Executive Summary Canada is not capturing the post-patent opportunity to supply its own medicines. While generic and equivalent medicines account for the majority of prescriptions dispensed in Canada, only a small proportion, estimated at 12% in 2022, are manufactured domestically. As a result, our country remains heavily reliant on multinational pharmaceutical companies for the supply of essential medicines. In a well-functioning system, the period following patent expiry should enable Canadian companies to compete, supply lower-cost alternatives and expand domestic production. In practice, this transition is not occurring. Market dynamics continue to favour large, non-Canadian firms, limiting competition and preventing domestic manufacturers from gaining meaningful market share. This dynamic also limits price competition, resulting in higher costs for public drug plans and Canadian patients, and limiting the savings that should be realized following patent expiry. This structural problem not only increases the risk of supply disruptions that can affect patient care, but also results in higher and unavoidable costs for the healthcare system at a time when governments can least afford unnecessary spending. This is not a question of capacity. Canadian companies exist, are growing and are already investing in domestic manufacturing. However, current market conditions do not provide the predictability or fairness required to support long-term investment in local production. The result is a structural weakness: Canada continues to rely on external suppliers even when

Name: [ORG] Brief on “Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty” – Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Introduction [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits the following brief to the
card_text: [ORG] Brief on “Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty” – Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Introduction [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits the following brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health regarding its study on “Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty.” This study comes at a pivotal moment for Canada’s life sciences sector, national security, and Canadian patients. The global pharmaceutical landscape is changing rapidly. The introduction of the most-favoured nation (MFN) drug pricing policy in the U.S., which aims to lower American prices and shift a greater share of innovation costs onto countries like Canada, has triggered a global chain reaction that is intensifying competition and influencing which countries get innovative medicines first. This move comes alongside President Trump’s recent executive order to impose a 100 per cent tariff on imported patented medicines and active ingredients. For Canada, this exposes a critical vulnerability: we have long benefited from U.S.- funded pharmaceutical innovation, while keeping our domestic drug prices low, ultimately limiting our availability of innovative medicines. Addressing this vulnerability starts with the fundamental premise that pharmaceutical sovereignty cannot happen in a vacuum. Any credible strategy must balance increased domestic capacity with the inherently global and interconnected nature of supply chains for innovative medicines. Canada has the foundation to get this right. Our innovative pharmaceutical industry is an economic engine that contributes $18.4 billion in economic impact, invests $3.2 billion in research and development, and supports over 110,000 high-value jobs. Underpinning this economic footprint are world-class researchers and academic institutions, and rigorous drug

card_text: Boehringer Ingelheim (Canada) Ltd/Ltée - Burlington, Ontario Standing Committee on Health Sixth Floor, [CONTACT] House of Commons Ottawa ON [CONTACT] Canada Boehringer Ingelheim (Canada) Ltd/Ltée Head, Government Affairs Subject: Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty On behalf of Boehringer Ingelheim (Canada) Ltd. (Boehringer), thank you for the opportunity to contribute to the House of Commons Health Committee’s (HESA’s) study on Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty. As a research‑driven biopharmaceutical company in Canada for over half a century, we see firsthand how the country’s regulatory and access systems shape the availability of medicines for patients. Every day, our teams work within these frameworks – submitting evidence, navigating multiple review bodies, and coordinating with federal and provincial partners. This experience gives us a practical understanding of where the system supports timely access and where it places unnecessary strain on patients, regulators, and innovators. The global environment has changed dramatically in recent years. Supply chains have become more fragile, scientific advances are accelerating, and countries are moving quickly to modernize their regulatory systems to keep pace. These shifts are occurring alongside a more complex geopolitical and trade landscape, where governments are reassessing how they secure access to essential goods, including medicines. International pricing initiatives, including the United States’ “Most Favored Nation” (MFN) drug pricing policy,1 have added another layer of uncertainty by increasing scrutiny on how countries make reimbursement decisions and how those decisions influence global launch and supply planning. card_text: May 5, 2026 Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Sixth Floor, [CONTACT] House of Commons Ottawa ON [CONTACT] Canada Subject: HESA study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Dear Committee Members, The [ORG] ([ORG]) appreciates the opportunity to contribute to the committee’s study on Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty. For Canadians living with rare diseases, the concept of sovereignty is not abstract. It is directly tied to whether patients can rely on stable, predictable access to the therapies they need, many of which have no therapeutic alternatives and are produced through highly specialized global supply chains. Rare disease medicines represent some of the most advanced scientific innovations available today. They are also among the most vulnerable to disruptions in manufacturing, regulatory delays and unpredictable market conditions. When access breaks down, patients often have no fallback options. For this reason, strengthening pharmaceutical sovereignty for rare disease therapies is essential to ensuring that Canada can meet the needs of its most vulnerable populations. Canada has taken important steps in recent years, including the launch of the National Drugs for Rare Diseases (DRD) Strategy and the development of the Biomanufacturing and Life Sciences Strategy (BLSS). These initiatives have created a foundation for improved access and greater resilience. However, Canada continues to face challenges that affect its ability to secure timely and reliable access to rare disease medicines. These include slower approval and reimbursement timelines than peer countries, limited domestic manufacturing capacity for specialized therapies and increasing global competition for investment and product launches. card_text: May 5, 2026 Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Sixth Floor, [CONTACT] House of Commons Ottawa ON [CONTACT] Canada Subject: HESA Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Dear Committee Members, On behalf of Alexion Pharma Canada Corp. (“Alexion”), thank you for the opportunity to contribute to the Committee’s study on Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty. For people living with rare and ultra‑rare diseases, reliable access to medicines is essential. Most rare disease therapies have no substitutes, limited global supply, and complex manufacturing processes. This makes patients more exposed to disruptions in global supply chains. Only 60% of rare disease therapies launched globally are approved in Canada,1 and public reimbursement can take years. The median wait from approval to public listing for rare disease medicines is 21 months, which is much longer than other comparable countries.2 These considerations are becoming more relevant as the global environment continues to evolve. International trade dynamics are shifting, tariff frameworks are being reassessed, and pricing policies in several jurisdictions, including the United States’ Most‑Favored Nation (MFN) approach, are becoming more closely linked. Against this backdrop, Canada could experience increasing challenges in securing early access to new medicines, particularly in smaller therapeutic areas where initial supply is limited and global allocation decisions are made well in advance. Pharmaceutical sovereignty for rare diseases therefore extends beyond domestic manufacturing capacity. It depends on Canada being an early, reliable, and competitive launch market. card_text: April 27, 2026 National Universal Pharmacare and Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submission by the [ORG] to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) as part if its study on pharmaceutical sovereignty. T he [ORG] is pleased to make this submission to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) as part of its study on Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty. We wish to draw your attention to the report of the National Pharmacare Committee of Experts established under C-64, the Pharmacare Act,i which was tabled in the House of Commons by Health Minister Marjorie Michel on November 21, 2025.ii The report underscores the importance of the federal government’s national universal pharmacare program. As committee chairperson Dr. Nav Persaud writes, “People die because medicines are still not included in Canada’s publicly funded health care system.” The eight recommendations from the Committee of Experts chart a path forward for the oneyear-old national universal pharmacare program, touching upon the pharmaceutical, socioeconomic, legislative, and fiscal future of the program. The Committee of Experts addresses Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty among its recommendations. Specifically, the committee urges the federal government to develop a national essential medicines strategy that ensures affordability and accessibility. “By implementing competitive procurement and strategic financing agreements, Canada can strengthen its health care system, safeguard supply chains, and promote domestic pharmaceutical production,” the Committee states in Recommendation 4. Name: Brief: Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submitted to the Standing Committee on Health on April 29, 2026
card_text: Brief: Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submitted to the Standing Committee on Health on April 29, 2026 Executive Summary [ORG] (CF Canada) is pleased to provide a brief to HESA regarding pharmaceutical sovereignty. Recent US pharmaceutical policy reforms and t threats of tariffs have created global instability and exposed structural vulnerabilities in Canada’s production and distribution of medicines, as well as in our drug review and reimbursement processes. For people living with cystic fibrosis (CF) and other rare diseases, these vulnerabilities may result in limited clinical trial access, delayed and/or reduced drug launches, and inequitable coverage of medicines across jurisdictions. Pharmaceutical sovereignty must be understood not as isolation or self-sufficiency, but as strategic resilience: the ability to secure timely access to innovative medicines, protect supply chains, and position Canada as a competitive jurisdiction for biomedical research, manufacturing, and early launch of and access to transformative therapies, including gene and cell therapies. For rare diseases like CF, where patient populations are small and drug development costs may be high, sovereignty must also mean alignment of global and domestic policies and processes, regulatory efficiency, and predictable access pathways. Drawing on evidence from HESA’s study on pharmaceutical sovereignty and CF Canada’s direct engagement with federal, provincial and territorial drug agencies, this brief outlines how Canada can strengthen pharmaceutical sovereignty in ways that prioritize patients with rare diseases like CF, while reinforcing Canada’s broader health and innovation objectives. Name: Built on U.S. Plasma: Canada’s Sovereignty Problem A Brief on Pharmaceutical Sovereignty in Plasma-Derived Therapies April 16, 2026
card_text: Built on U.S. Plasma: Canada’s Sovereignty Problem A Brief on Pharmaceutical Sovereignty in Plasma-Derived Therapies April 16, 2026 Dear Members of the HESA Committee: This brief concerns pharmaceutical sovereignty with respect to plasma-derived medicinal products (PDMPs), especially immunoglobulin. These medicines are essential for patients with immune deficiencies and other serious conditions, and they depend on a very large and reliable supply of collected plasma. Canada remains heavily dependent on plasma collected in the United States. If Canada is to be sovereign over this part of the pharmaceutical supply chain, there is no feasible path apart from welcoming a larger domestic commercial plasma collection sector. This dependence is more serious given rising demand for immunoglobulin, one of the medicines made from plasma. Our need for this medicine has nearly doubled over the past ten years, and is on track to nearly double again over the next ten years. I write as an ethicist and public policy scholar who has conducted extensive comparative research on plasma collection, donor safety, and plasma self-sufficiency across Canada, the United States, Australia, New Zealand and Europe, including the UK. Sincerely, Peter M. Jaworski, Ph.D. Professor of Ethics in Strategy, Ethics, Economics, and Public Policy, McDonough School of Business, Georgetown University Director, Georgetown Blood and Plasma Research Group Member of the Network of Experts, International Patient Organization for Primary Immunodeficiencies EXECUTIVE SUMMARY ● Canada is not sovereign over its plasma medicine supply chain. [CONTACT], Suite 204 Mississauga, ON [CONTACT] www.drreddys.com Written Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health for the Study on Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submitted on April 16, 2026 Dr. Reddy’s Laboratories Canada Inc. appreciates the opportunity to provide input to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health as part of its study on Pharmaceutical Sovereignty. Introduction Pharmaceutical sovereignty is a shared objective: ensuring that Canadians have reliable, timely, and affordable access to essential medicines, including during periods of global disruption. From the perspective of a global generic pharmaceutical manufacturer with a strong Canadian footprint, Dr. Reddy’s believes this objective is best achieved through a balanced and pragmatic approach that strengthens domestic capacity while fully leveraging trusted global manufacturing and regulatory partnerships, supported by an efficient and predictable regulatory environment. Dr. Reddy’s Laboratories offers the following recommendations on securing Canada’s Drug Sovereignty. 1. Place access to generics at the centre of pharmaceutical sovereignty discussions. 2. Pursue a balanced strategy that integrates domestic and global manufacturing. 3. Work towards more predictable Health Canada approval timelines. 4. Promote supplier diversity rather than concentration. 5. Leverage trade and international regulatory cooperation. The Central Role of Generics in Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Generics play an essential role in Canada’s health system, delivering the majority of prescription volumes and generating significant cost savings for public and private payers. card_text: [CONTACT] West Toronto, ON [CONTACT] Canada doctorswithoutborders.ca medecinssansfrontieres.ca March 16, 2026 Dear Members of the Standing Committee on Health (HESA), RE: [ORG]/Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) Submission – Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Introduction Tackling the topic of Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty necessitates recognizing that Canada is affected by events beyond its borders over which it does not have full control, whether the emergence and spread of a new disease that makes Canadians ill, or access to the medicines that can make them better. Similarly, decisions made in Canada, such as the recent cuts to global health under the federal international assistance budget, can have impacts on both the spread of disease and access to medicines abroad. As a medical humanitarian organization operating in over 70 countries, MSF witnesses firsthand the impact of outbreaks and epidemics as they transcend national borders, as well as the deadly consequences of inequities in access to medicines, vaccines and other health tools. The submission below emphasizes the fact addressing Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty must look outward as well as inward to effectively address Canada’s place within the larger world. In making this submission, MSF expresses concern that the Government of Canada has increasingly framed pharmaceutical development and production as an economic driver rather than as a means to benefit society across numerous metrics by keeping people healthy. Name: Pharmaceutical Sovereignty and Insulin Supply in Canada Submission to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) by Independent Voices for Safe & Effective Drugs
org: Independent Voices for Safe and Effective Drugs
card_text: Pharmaceutical Sovereignty and Insulin Supply in Canada Submission to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) by Independent Voices for Safe & Effective Drugs March 13, 2026 Executive Summary Insulin is one of the most important medicines in modern healthcare. For the 640,000 Canadians living with diabetes who need it, uninterrupted access to insulin is essential for survival. For those with Type 1 diabetes, an auto-immune disease, there are no substitutes for insulin and without it they will die within days or weeks. Despite insulin’s importance—and its discovery in Canada—we currently have no domestic manufacturing capacity for this medicine. Instead, Canadians rely entirely on three foreign corporations that control 95% of the global supply of insulin. Recent developments in the global insulin market, including the discontinuation of multiple insulin products for commercial reasons, illustrate the dangers of relying exclusively on global pharmaceutical supply chains for essential medicines. The World Health Organisation estimates that half of the world’s population who need insulin either cannot afford it or have no access to supplies. Other jurisdictions have begun responding to these risks by strengthening domestic pharmaceutical manufacturing capacity and treating certain medicines, including insulin, as strategic assets. Canada is not currently among these. Until the mid-1980s, however, Canada did maintain domestic production of insulin and other biologics through publicly owned Connaught Laboratories. Re-establishing domestic insulin manufacturing capacity—preferably under public ownership and accountability—would strengthen Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty while ensuring secure access to one of the country’s most important medicines. card_text: From registration to retention: Supporting Internationally Educated Nurses through quality bridging education and integration support Submitted to House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Parliament of Canada Submitted by Dr. Jean Daniel Jacob Executive Director [ORG] Contact Information Email: [CONTACT] Phone: [CONTACT] Website: www.casn.ca Address: 200-[CONTACT]., Ottawa, ON, [CONTACT] Submitted on December 2, 2025 A Note on Terminology This briefing note refers to the nursing professions in Canada, which include licensed practical nurses (LPNs), registered psychiatric nurses (RPNs), registered nurses (RNs), and nurse practitioners (NPs). In Ontario, the designation for diploma-prepared nurses is registered practical nurse. In this note, we use LPNs when referring to practical nurses across Canada. Background Concerns surrounding the domestic production of nurses are not new. In 2018, forecasting models predicted that Canada would face a shortage of 117,600 nurses by 2030 (Scheffler & Arnold, 2019). The COVID-19 pandemic introduced conditions that accelerated retirements and other attrition from the nursing workforce. Nursing programs have increased seats in response to the rising demand for LPNs, RNs, and NPs. For example, from 2009–2020, program seats in entry-to-practice programs for RNs remained stable, admitting an average of 15,696 students annually ([ORG] [CASN], in press). A notable 12% increase occurred between 2021 and 2024, with programs admitting 18,389 students (see Figure 1). During this period, seats in NP programs increased by 41%. The current and future shortage of veterinarians in Canada poses a critical risk to Canadians’ health. Canada needs a dedicated national clinical proficiency testing centre for internationally trained veterinarians to ensure the country has a sufficient workforce to meet our needs now and into the future. BRINGING TOGETHER HUMAN, ANIMAL, AND ECOSYSTEM HEALTH The health of humans, domestic and wild animals, plants, and the wider environment (including ecosystems) are closely linked and interdependent. Collaborating across sectors and disciplines to protect health, address health challenges such as the emergence of infectious diseases, antimicrobial resistance, and food safety and promote the health and integrity of our ecosystems is called a "One Health" approach.i Because of that interdependence, Canada's veterinarians form the foundation of full spectrum of disease control from prevention to detection, preparedness, response and management. They are a critical link in protecting public health in Canada and providing Canada's contribution to global health security. card_text: Written Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health regarding the Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals Submitted by [ORG] About [ORG] [ORG] is a group of companies with business interests in skills training and workforce development, media and communications, and global market facilitation. Our vision is to inform, educate and activate global citizens to make a worldwide impact. We do this by providing our clients with innovative, inclusive, and scalable training, business and communications solutions that prepare our clients to address global challenges. Recommendations: Key federal departments, including Health Canada, Employment and Social Development Canada, Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada, Global Affairs Canada, and Export Development Canada, should begin exploring to support a shift towards the use of international TVET to address the shortage of PSWs and other key technical and vocational key healthcare professions, including by: • engaging with experts and potential GSC operators, • examining opportunities to provide both financial and diplomatic support, and • evaluating possible changes to Canada’s immigration system to ensure alignment with the concept of international TVET and the GSC model. Overview Canada is at a pivotal moment. Our country is facing critical shortages of health care professionals and other skilled workers. These gaps are only expected to grow in the coming years as a result of Canada’s aging population, significant reductions in temporary foreign workers, and growing competition for skilled immigration across the Global North. Canada must take action to address these challenges.

card_text: Written Submission to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals Systemic Barriers Facing Internationally Trained Dentists (ITDs) in Canada and the Need for Federal Oversight of the National Dental Examining Board (NDEB) Submitted by Seyedeh Fereshteh Morsali Internationally Trained Dentist (ITD) November 2025 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY Internationally Trained Dentists (ITDs) in Canada face severe and well-documented barriers in their attempts to enter the dental workforce, despite Canada’s urgent need for qualified health-care professionals. The National Dental Examining Board of Canada (NDEB), which oversees the equivalency and licensing process, has created a system that is widely perceived by candidates as inconsistent, subjective, and lacking the transparency expected of a national regulatory body. These issues are particularly evident in the NDECC (Skills Exam), where candidates report unpredictable scoring, unrealistic clinical conditions, and an exam structure that does not reflect real-world dentistry. The consequences are significant: highly trained, experienced dentists—many with over a decade of clinical practice—are repeatedly failing an exam that often appears to reward inexperience rather than competence. Candidates face financial strain, emotional distress, career instability, and prolonged under-employment, frequently being forced into low-skill or low-wage jobs despite possessing advanced professional qualifications. These systemic issues affect thousands of internationally trained professionals across Canada and directly undermine federal goals related to immigration, labour market integration, and health-care capacity. card_text: Submission to The Standing Committee on Health Study: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals Submitted by the [ORG] November 24, 2025 Introduction Canada’s ability to deliver timely, high-quality healthcare to children and youth increasingly depends on the strength of its highly specialized pediatric workforce. Today, that workforce is atrisk. Across the country, pediatricians and pediatric subspecialists—whose expertise is essential when children require preventive care, specialized treatments, or coordinated diagnosis and management of complex conditions—are in short supply. In addition to our important national focus on primary care, we must also recognize increasing subspecialty shortages which are directly limiting access to medically necessary care and compromising long-term health outcomes for Canadian infants, children and teenagers. This challenge is not abstract. More than 8 million children and youth currently live in Canada, a number expected to grow by 24% over the next fifty years. Yet across many jurisdictions, children now wait longer than adults for essential care, not because their needs are less urgent, but because the workforce trained to meet those needs is too small, aging, and increasingly stretched to meet the needs of an increasingly complex population of kids. Why Pediatric Workforce Sustainability Matters Children are not tiny adults. Children have unique physiological, developmental, and social needs that require dedicated training and coordinated care. Pediatric subspecialties—such as neonatology, pediatric neurology, gastroenterology, cardiology, oncology, and developmental pediatrics—form highly interdependent systems and are not inter-changeable with subspecialists who care for adults. card_text: [ORG]: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare Nov 25, 2025 Executive Summary Recent changes to immigration policy are beginning to impact [ORG]’s ability to sustain care delivery. With over 8,000 staff providing approximately 25,000 healthcare visits daily, [ORG] has relied on more than 650 temporary foreign workers to address persistent workforce shortages and expand services in high-need areas. However, a growing number of work permit renewal denials now threaten this critical staffing stream. If the trend continues, it could significantly reduce [ORG]’s capacity to meet rising demand - particularly as Canada’s aging population accelerates. The potential loss of these workers would result in loss of around 1,800 health care interactions daily, impacting thousands of Canadians. [ORG] urges this committee to recommend that the House of Commons address the urgent need for policy changes to support the continued use of temporary foreign workers in the healthcare system. Impacts of Immigration Policy Changes Canada is experiencing a persistent health human resources shortage that is placing growing pressure on the home and community care sector. As the population ages and demand for services rises, the limited supply of trained healthcare professionals, particularly nurses and personal support workers, is making it increasingly difficult to sustain high-quality care in the community. Recruitment and retention challenges, compounded by regional disparities and workforce burnout, have led many providers to rely on temporary or internationally trained workers to fill critical gaps. card_text: November 19, 2025 Catherine Ngando Edimo Clerk of the committee – HESA [CONTACT] Re: HESA Committee Study on the Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals On behalf of the [ORG] ([ORG]), I would like to thank HESA for this study and the opportunity to provide input in writing. Founded in 1995, the [ORG] ([ORG]) is the alliance of Canada’s pharmacy regulators – the 13 provincial and territorial pharmacy regulatory authorities (PRAs) across the country as well as the Canadian Forces Health Services. [ORG]’s Members regulate the practice of pharmacy in their respective jurisdictions in Canada, and their primary mandate is to protect and serve the public interest. [ORG] facilitates collaboration and national approaches among its Members as they address common issues in the practice of pharmacy in Canada. The provincial and territorial PRAs govern the pharmacy professions so that they best support the public in realizing optimal health outcomes. As part of that mandate, PRAs license pharmacy professionals (pharmacists and pharmacy technicians), set required standards and codes of ethics for pharmacy practice, and investigate and resolve complaints in their respective jurisdictions. In addition to their core work of establishing and implementing licensure pathways, and ensuring adherence to professional standards, PRAs also explore opportunities that would allow them to better serve the public interest. Pharmacy professionals are the third largest group of health professionals in Canada, after nurses and physicians0F1, with over 50,600 registered/licensed pharmacists and over 11,500 registered/licensed pharmacy technicians across the country.

card_text: Briefly about me: I am a Canadian citizen. I mention this because Canada is the only home I’ve known; I understand Canadian culture and I do not require a visa. I am a healthcare provider so I am familiar with the ethical framework, the Health Professional Act and the Canadian healthcare system. I have experience working in remote BC communities. I understand the limitations and increased workload required within small communities, where the need is great. As well, rural providers have long waitlists. I self funded my education to study a Caribbean medical school. I completed third year hospital clerkships in USA, which is an approved jurisdiction. I am currently in my fourth year. I completed rotations in USA as well as rural and urban Canada. I am applying to internal medicine residency in both Canada and America, via CARMS and ERAS, respectively. My partner works in in BC, he loves his work and his employer loves him. He is originally from Europe, he has lived in various European countries, Australia, Caribbean, USA and Canada. I am very familiar with the immigration process. Both of us hope that I match in Canada so we can raise a family here and continue to call it home, but we have decided that we will settle in whichever country I match because that will require the least amount of investment. card_text: ® Written Submission to The Standing Committee on Health Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals Submitted by the [ORG] November 2025 Category Based Selection: Considerations for Dentistry Canada is currently facing a significant health human resource challenge, and the Government of Canada is taking decisive action to attract the expertise and talent required to meet the health needs of Canadians. One important initiative has been the prioritization of health-related occupations through the Express Entry system. While this approach appropriately seeks to strengthen Canada’s health workforce, the inclusion of dentistry in dedicated rounds of invitations for healthcare professionals may unintentionally create unrealistic expectations among prospective immigrants regarding their ability to obtain licensure and practise as dentists in Canada. Under an Act of Parliament, the [ORG] ([ORG]) has a statutory obligation to certify only those candidates who meet the established national standard of competence. All individuals— regardless of citizenship or country of education—must successfully complete the [ORG]’s Certification Process prior to licensure in Canada. Canada’s dental profession maintains reciprocal agreements with a limited number of countries, including the United States, Ireland, Australia, and New Zealand. These agreements recognize the equivalency of dental education and facilitate licensure for graduates of accredited programs in those jurisdictions. However, internationally trained dentists (ITDs) from countries without such reciprocal recognition must demonstrate equivalency through one of two routes: 1. card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals Submitted by: [ORG] ([ORG]) Date: November 5th, 2025 Executive Summary Canada’s long-term care (LTC) sector is experiencing a severe and growing workforce crisis that directly impacts the quality of care for older adults and the overall sustainability of the health system. As the population ages—particularly with the number of Canadians aged 85 and older expected to double by 2040—demand for LTC will rise dramatically. Yet, according to Statistics Canada (2024), there are already over 25,000 vacant LTC positions across the country1. Domestic recruitment and training initiatives remain essential but will take time to have an effect. Immediate workforce stabilization requires federal leadership through targeted immigration policy, enhanced settlement supports and streamlined administrative processes. [ORG] recommends: 1. Include LTC homes in federal settlement funding programs to ensure that employers, newcomers and their families have the support needed to successfully integrate, further supporting their retention. 2. Expand and simplify immigration pathways—for example, by creating a healthcarespecific pilot for LTC. This could build on strengths and lessons learned from the Home Care Worker Immigration Pilot and the Atlantic Immigration Program to attract and retain internationally educated health professionals specifically interested in working in LTC . 3. Create a healthcare-specific work permit that is Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA) exempt. card_text: Written submission to the Standing Committee on Health Addressing Workforce Challenges in Dermatology Submitted by the [ORG] November 4, 2025 Introduction Canada's dermatologist community issued early warnings in the 2000s regarding a critical shortage of dermatologists1. The profession aimed to pre-empt the significant demographic shifts now evident in 2025, specifically a rapidly increasing aging population alongside a dermatology workforce shortage2. Despite these warnings, proactive solutions were not implemented, leaving us in a far more difficult position today. The health human resource (HHR) shortage is one that is prevalent throughout the entire Canadian health care system and extends globally. HHR recruitment and retention challenges have been building for years and were significantly intensified by the pressures of COVID-193. Across the system, gaps in workforce planning, training capacity, and provider well-being are straining the delivery of timely care. Dermatology is a critical component of an effective health care system and has been directly affected by these system pressures. The skin is the body’s largest organ and plays a vital role in overall health, providing a first line of defense against infection and environmental hazards. When skin is diseased, the impacts can be severe. Conditions like skin cancer require rapid diagnosis and treatment to prevent death, while non-lifethreatening conditions such as eczema, psoriasis, or severe acne can lead to chronic pain, physical discomfort, and serious mental health and systemic consequences4. Name: How the Government of Canada can increase the supply of family doctors
study_title: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals
card_text: How the Government of Canada can increase the supply of family doctors Overview Canada has a primary care crisis. Some 6.5 million Canadians do not have a family doctor. The federal government can rapidly and cost-effectively help mitigate this crisis by funding Practice Ready Assessment (PRA) programs across Canada. PRA programs provide an accelerated pathway to licensure for internationally-trained physicians (ITPs), thousands of whom are currently living in Canada as permanent residents and citizens. ITPs have completed their medical school and residency and have practiced medicine abroad. They have met all Canadian medical licensing requirements except for one: a competency assessment. This can be provided through a PRA which can be completed in 12 weeks or less. Successful completion of a PRA indicates that the ITP has demonstrated the ability to provide medical care at Canadian standards. Proposal We propose the federal government fund 4,500 PRA spots across Canada over a 4-year period. Given the average family physician sees 1,500 patients per year, this would give each currently waiting Canadian access to a family doctor by the end of the 4-year period. The proposal is efficient, since becoming licensed through a PRA is much quicker than the 2-year period to complete a medical residency. The proposal is also more costeffective than funding residencies–which cost about $340,000 per spot. The average cost for each 12-week PRA is only $35,000. card_text: [ORG] Submission to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals October 2025 Underutilized Health Care Skills in Canada Health care systems across Canada are in crisis, facing critical shortages of qualified health professionals to respond to increased demands for care, particularly in small and mid-sized communities. The federal government anticipates a shortage of 117,600 nurses by 2030 and 20,000 family doctors by 2031. Meanwhile, thousands of internationally educated health professionals (IEHPs) living in Canada are ready and qualified to help. Many are Canadian citizens and permanent residents. However, systemic barriers to licensure, certification, and workforce integration continue to limit their ability to contribute to their full potential. Statistics Canada data suggest that roughly 47% of internationally educated health professionals are either unemployed or working outside their field. The reasons for this are well understood and well-documented. They include insufficient access to training positions (for example, medical residency training), barriers to maintaining recent practice experience, high fees associated with assessments and licensing processes, insufficient access to bridging or laddering pathways, lengthy timelines, and the complexity of information about licensing pathways. Canada invites health care professionals to the country through our immigration system and invests in programs to support their integration through federal and provincial immigration dollars, as well as health care, employment and education funds.
